content.text: Production of goods in large quantities after processing from raw materials to more valuable products is called manufacturing. The economic strength of a country is measured by the development of manufacturing industries.

content.title: Why Manufacturing is the backbone of development:
items: Modernises agriculture, reducing dependence on agricultural income.
Eradicates unemployment and poverty through job creation in secondary and tertiary sectors.
Reduces regional disparities by establishing industries in tribal and backward areas.
Earns foreign exchange through the export of manufactured goods.
Transforms raw materials into high-value finished goods, leading to national prosperity.

content.title: Interdependence
content.text: Agriculture and industry move hand in hand. Agro-industries raise agricultural productivity by providing pumps, fertilizers, and tools, while depending on agriculture for raw materials.
Industrial locations are complex and influenced by the availability of raw materials, labour, capital, power, and market. Government policies and specialised labour also play a role.

content.title: Agglomeration Economies
content.text: Many industries tend to come together to make use of the advantages offered by urban centres (banking, insurance, transport, financial advice). These are called agglomeration economies.

rows: SAIL; TISCO; Oil India Ltd; Sugar (Maharashtra)

content.text: The textile industry is self-reliant and complete in the value chain (raw material to high-value products). It contributes significantly to production, employment, and foreign exchange.

content.title: Cotton and Jute Highlights:
items: Cotton: First successful mill in Mumbai (1854). Concentrated in Maharashtra/Gujarat due to raw cotton and moist climate.
Jute: India is the largest producer/second exporter. First mill near Kolkata (Rishra, 1855). Most mills are along the Hugli river.

content.text: India stands second in sugar production but first in Gur and Khandsari. This industry is seasonal and ideally suited to the cooperative sector.

content.title: The Southward Shift
content.text: In recent years, mills are shifting to southern and western states (especially Maharashtra) because the cane has higher sucrose content and the cooler climate ensures a longer crushing season.
The basic industry upon which all others depend. Heavy industry due to bulky raw materials (Iron ore, coking coal, limestone in 4:2:1 ratio). Chhotanagpur Plateau has the maximum concentration.

content.title: Key Features:
items: Light, corrosion-resistant, malleable, and a good conductor.
Raw material: Bauxite (bulky, dark reddish rock).
Location Factor: Regular electricity supply and cheap source of raw material.

content.text: Chemical industry is its own largest consumer. Inorganic chemicals include acids and alkalies; Organic includes petrochemicals. The Fertilizer industry focuses on Nitrogenous (N), Phosphatic (P), and Potash (K) - the latter being entirely imported.

Cement is essential for construction; first plant in Chennai (1904). Automobiles grew rapidly after liberalisation. Bengaluru is the 'Electronic Capital' of India, with Software Technology Parks (STPs) providing high-speed data connectivity to software experts.

content.title: Industrial impact:
items: Air: Caused by SO2, CO, and smoke from chemical/paper factories.
Water: Organic/inorganic wastes, dyes, and heavy metals (lead, mercury).
Thermal: Draining hot water from factories into rivers before cooling.
Land/Soil: Correlated with water pollution; dumping hazardous glass/garbage renders soil useless.
Noise: Machineries, generators, and pneumatic drills causing stress and hearing impairment.

content.title: Treatment Phases:
items: Tertiary: Biological, chemical, and physical (recycling wastewater).

content.title: NTPC - The Green Model
content.text: NTPC (ISO 14001) follows a proactive approach for environment preservation through equipment upgrades, ash utilisation, green belts, and ecological monitoring.

glossary.definition: Production of goods in large quantities after processing from raw materials to more valuable products.
Industries which supply their products as raw materials to manufacture other goods (e.g., Iron and Steel).
The advantages (banking, insurance, etc.) for industries resulting from their concentration in or around urban centres.
A dark reddish coloured rock that is the raw material used in aluminium smelting.
Liquid waste or sewage discharged into a river or the sea.
National Manufacturing Competitiveness Council; aimed at improving industrial productivity.

summary_points: Manufacturing is the backbone of economic development, modernising agriculture and earning vital foreign exchange.
Industrial locations are determined by raw materials, labour, and agglomeration economies provided by urban centres.
Industries are classified into agro-based and mineral-based, further divided by role, capital, and ownership.
The textile industry (Cotton and Jute) is a major contributor to employment, while Iron and Steel serves as the basic industry for machinery.
Industries like Aluminium, Chemicals, and IT (Bengaluru) are growing rapidly, though industrial pollution remains a critical challenge.
Sustainability depends on controlling environmental degradation through multi-phase effluent treatment and proactive management (e.g., NTPC).
